text: The surgical setup includes four 8mm robotic trocars and one 5mm assistant trocar positioned for the bedside surgeon.
After docking the robotic system, the surgeon incises the peritoneum and opens the aorta mesenteric dindo using monopolar scissors and bipolar forceps.
The surgeon exposes the inferior mesenteric vein at the inferior border of the pancreas, ligates it with Hem-o-lok clips, and transects the vein.
The surgeon continues dissection through the embryological planes, carefully preserving the left gonadal vein during the robotic left colectomy procedure.

The surgeon isolates the left colic artery after demonstrating the inferior mesenteric artery. The left colic artery is ligated with clips and transected at its origin, while the superior rectal artery is preserved.
The surgeon separates the transverse mesocolon from the anterior surface of the pancreas, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the dissection.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac and continues dissection towards the splenic flexure, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue planes in this region.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection inferiorly, carefully separating the left mesocolon from the Gerota's fascia and taut fascia while preserving the left ureter, gonadal vessels, and autonomic nerves.
The surgeon continues dissection up to the spleen, fully mobilizing the splenic flexure by separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon focuses on the branches of the inferior mesenteric artery, examining and preparing them for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon isolates the sigmoid artery, ligates it with clips, and transects it at its origin, completing the vascular control in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates the left colic vein with clips and transects it, completing the vascular dissection in the region.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the left mesocolon, preparing it for the anticipated transection line, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the distal dromones artery, ligates it with clips, and transects it, completing the vascular dissection in the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon focuses on the middle colic vessels located at the transverse mesocolon, preparing for further dissection or ligation.
The surgeon demonstrates the middle colic vessels from their origin, carefully exposing them from the surrounding tissue within the transverse mesocolon.
The surgeon dissects the left branch of the middle colic artery from its origin, ligates it with clips, and transects it.
The surgeon dissects the splenic colic and gastrocolic ligaments, facilitating the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon fully mobilizes and dissects the greater omentum at the lower border of the stomach, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure, with the surrounding tissue visibly separated and the flexure fully freed from its attachments.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the distal colon using indocyanine green dye, then transects the left colon with a robotic stapler.
The surgeon prepares the proximal colon for transection, checking its perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) dye.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the colon, completing the transection process.
The surgeon places traction sutures for the anastomosis, followed by colatamine on both sides to facilitate the placement of the stapler.
The surgeon creates a tension-free, side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis using two robotic stapler fires, connecting the transected sections of the colon.
The surgeon closes the anastomotic defect using a two-layer suturing technique, ensuring secure closure of the previously created anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the inner layer of the anastomosis using a running 3-0 V-Loc suture, securing the tissue with continuous stitching.
The surgeon applies interrupted single silk sutures to reinforce the second layer of the anastomosis.
The surgeon demonstrates the blood flow of the anastomosis using indocyanine green dye, which highlights the vascular perfusion in the anastomotic area.
